title: Base reference calls Base version
prompt_md:
## The problem

When a member function is called through a `Base&` reference or `Base*` pointer, C++ resolves it using the *static type* of the reference/pointer -- not the real runtime type of the object. Build a small `Animal` hierarchy that exposes this behavior.

## What to build

Create a base class `Animal` that stores a name and has a `speak()` member returning `"..."`, plus derived classes `Cat` (speaks `"Meow"`) and `Dog` (speaks `"Woof"`). None of these functions are `virtual`. For each animal you read, call `speak()` two ways on the *same* object: directly on the derived object, and through an `Animal&` bound to it. Print both results on one line.

## Input

- Line 1: integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10).
- Next `N` lines: `<type> <name>` where `<type>` is `cat` or `dog` and `<name>` is a single word.

## Output

One line per animal, in input order:
```
<name>: direct=<derived sound> ref=<sound via Animal&>
```
Because the calls are non-virtual, the `ref=` value is always `...`.

test_cases.label: Mixed cats and dogs
test_cases.stdin:
3
cat Felix
dog Rex
cat Tom
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Felix: direct=Meow ref=...
Rex: direct=Woof ref=...
Tom: direct=Meow ref=...

test_cases.label: Three cats then a dog
test_cases.stdin:
4
cat A
cat B
cat C
dog D
test_cases.expected_stdout:
A: direct=Meow ref=...
B: direct=Meow ref=...
C: direct=Meow ref=...
D: direct=Woof ref=...

test_cases.label: Two dogs
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Max: direct=Woof ref=...
Duke: direct=Woof ref=...

title: Function taking Base& hides derived behavior
prompt_md:
## The problem

A function parameter of type `const Shape&` can accept any derived shape, but a non-virtual member function called on that parameter always runs the `Shape` version -- the derived override is invisible. Demonstrate this.

## What to build

Create a base class `Shape` that stores a name; its `describe()` returns `"<name>: generic"`. Derive `Circle` (stores an int radius; `describe()` returns `"<name>: circle r=<radius>"`) and `Square` (stores an int side; `describe()` returns `"<name>: square s=<side>"`). None of these are `virtual`. Also write a free function `report` that takes a `const Shape&` and prints what `describe()` returns through that reference.

For each shape, print two lines: first the string returned by calling `describe()` directly on the derived object, then the line printed by `report`.

## Input

- Line 1: integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10).
- Next `N` lines: `circle <name> <radius>` or `square <name> <side>` (radius/side are integers).

## Output

Two lines per shape, in input order: the direct `describe()` result, then the `report` result (which is always the generic `Shape` form).

test_cases.label: Circle and square
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Disk: circle r=5
Disk: generic
Box: square s=4
Box: generic

test_cases.label: Two squares then a circle
test_cases.stdin:
3
square A 2
square B 7
circle C 3
test_cases.expected_stdout:
A: square s=2
A: generic
B: square s=7
B: generic
C: circle r=3
C: generic
